prompt: From 2020 to 2050, how many elderly people will there be in Japan? What is their consumption potential across various aspects such as clothing, food, housing, and transportation? Based on population projections, elderly consumer willingness, and potential changes in their consumption habits, please produce a market size analysis report for the elderly demographic.
article:
## Bottom line

Japan’s elderly consumer market is large already and becomes more central to domestic demand even if elderly headcount growth slows after the 2040s. Japan had **about 36.0 million people aged 65+ in 2020**, equal to **28.6%** of the population, and the official 2023 national projection indicates that the 65+ population keeps rising into the early 2040s, peaking at **39.53 million in 2043** before easing slightly thereafter [[1]](https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/e/zenkoku_e2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf)(https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/e/zenkoku_e2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf) [[77]](https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/kokusei/2020/summary/pdf/all.pdf)(https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/kokusei/2020/summary/pdf/all.pdf). That is an increase of roughly **3.5 million people**, or about **9.7%**, from 2020 to the peak. In the retrieved 2050 materials, the exact point estimate is not perfectly aligned across a national-table extract and a regional-projection summary, but the safest conclusion is that Japan will still have **roughly 39–40 million elderly people in 2050**, with the elderly share at **about 38%** of the population [[1]](https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/e/zenkoku_e2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf)(https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/e/zenkoku_e2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf) [[8]](https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/j/zenkoku2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf)(https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/j/zenkoku2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf) [[22]](https://www.ipss.go.jp/syoushika/bunken/DATA/pdf/24800106.pdf)(https://www.ipss.go.jp/syoushika/bunken/DATA/pdf/24800106.pdf). The commercial implication is not just “more seniors”; it is that the elderly account for a rising share of a shrinking national market.

The internal composition of that population matters as much as the total. By **2024**, Japan had **36.24 million** people aged 65+, including **15.47 million aged 65–74** and **20.78 million aged 75+**, so the later-old segment was already larger than the younger-old segment [[4]](https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2025/pdf/2025.pdf)(https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2025/pdf/2025.pdf). That changes demand in a way that simple population totals do not capture: a market weighted toward 75+ and 85+ buys less like a newly retired couple and more like a household that values reliability, delivery, safety, low-friction interfaces, and support at home.

The key commercial point is that **elderly market growth is not a simple volume story**. Broad **senior-household consumption** was estimated at **¥55.7 trillion in 2023** and is projected by Mizuho to reach **¥60.5 trillion in 2040 in real terms**, even as **all-generation consumption falls from ¥157.4 trillion to ¥150.9 trillion** over the same period [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf). But this is a **modest real expansion of about 8.6% over 17 years**, not a demographic boom. The growth is concentrated in **food, housing, transport/access, and support-linked consumption**, not in a broad rise in discretionary spending [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf) [[43]](https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/kakei/pdf/summary.pdf)(https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/kakei/pdf/summary.pdf) [[107]](https://www.mitsui.com/mgssi/en/report/detail/__icsFiles/afieldfile/2023/08/30/2307k_suzuki_e_2.pdf)(https://www.mitsui.com/mgssi/en/report/detail/__icsFiles/afieldfile/2023/08/30/2307k_suzuki_e_2.pdf). The biggest structural shifts are: **more single elderly households**, **more 75+ and 85+ consumers**, **more urban concentration in absolute numbers**, and **more rural access problems**, all of which push demand toward **convenience, home-based services, barrier-free housing, mobility support, and smaller-format food solutions** [[16]](https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-data/h02201/)(https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-data/h02201/) [[17]](https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/japans-elderly-population-living-alone-jump-47-by-2050-research-2024-04-12/)(https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/japans-elderly-population-living-alone-jump-47-by-2050-research-2024-04-12/) [[18]](https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20240415/p2a/00m/0na/007000c)(https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20240415/p2a/00m/0na/007000c) [[23]](https://english.news.cn/asiapacific/20231225/a28368635362479ca342ccbf850ee3af/c.html)(https://english.news.cn/asiapacific/20231225/a28368635362479ca342ccbf850ee3af/c.html) [[97]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/page/content/001906598.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/page/content/001906598.pdf) [[100]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001892310.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001892310.pdf) [[114]](https://openjicareport.jica.go.jp/pdf/1000048192.pdf)(https://openjicareport.jica.go.jp/pdf/1000048192.pdf).

A decision-useful summary is:

1. **Food is the largest spend pool and the most defensible mainstream opportunity.**
2. **Housing is the fastest structural grower among the four target categories because aging-in-place creates retrofit and support demand.**
3. **Transport is really an access market, where delivery, assisted mobility, taxis, and on-demand services substitute for one another.**
4. **Clothing is the weakest broad category but still offers high-value functional niches, especially adaptive and easy-dressing products.**

## Scope, assumptions, and market-sizing approach

This report uses **65+** as the core definition of “elderly,” because that is the standard used in Japan’s official demographic and household projections, while also separating **65–74**, **75+**, and highlighting **85+** where behavior and care needs change materially [[4]](https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2025/pdf/2025.pdf)(https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2025/pdf/2025.pdf) [[5]](https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2024/pdf/2024.pdf)(https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2024/pdf/2024.pdf) [[16]](https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-data/h02201/)(https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-data/h02201/). For **population**, the unit is persons; for **market size**, the most decision-useful unit is **households headed by someone aged 65+**, because the strongest published category-level spending estimates are constructed that way [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf). This distinction is important. A person-based lens is best for demand potential; a head-of-household lens is best for household expenditure modeling; and the two are not identical, because some elderly people live in households headed by younger family members.

The report distinguishes three layers of market size [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf) [[138]](https://www.fuji-keizai.co.jp/press/detail.html)(https://www.fuji-keizai.co.jp/press/detail.html) [[139]](https://www.care-show.com/prediction2025/)(https://www.care-show.com/prediction2025/) [[141]](https://www.jri.co.jp/MediaLibrary/file/pdf/company/release/2025/0221.pdf)(https://www.jri.co.jp/MediaLibrary/file/pdf/company/release/2025/0221.pdf):

1. **Broad elderly consumption market**: ordinary spending by households headed by people aged 65+, across mainstream categories such as food, housing, clothing, and transport/communications [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf).
2. **Elderly-oriented submarkets**: specialized products or services for older people, such as adaptive apparel, elderly-oriented foods, meal delivery, serviced senior housing, or assistive devices [[138]](https://www.fuji-keizai.co.jp/press/detail.html)(https://www.fuji-keizai.co.jp/press/detail.html) [[139]](https://www.care-show.com/prediction2025/)(https://www.care-show.com/prediction2025/) [[141]](https://www.jri.co.jp/MediaLibrary/file/pdf/company/release/2025/0221.pdf)(https://www.jri.co.jp/MediaLibrary/file/pdf/company/release/2025/0221.pdf) [[153]](https://www.datamintelligence.com/research-report/japan-assistive-devices-market)(https://www.datamintelligence.com/research-report/japan-assistive-devices-market).
3. **Access-enabling spending**: spending that substitutes for direct category consumption, such as delivery replacing shopping trips, or mobility support enabling retail access [[87]](https://www.maff.go.jp/primaff/seika/fsc/faccess/attach/pdf/1503_sum.pdf)(https://www.maff.go.jp/primaff/seika/fsc/faccess/attach/pdf/1503_sum.pdf) [[147]](https://kouseikyoku.mhlw.go.jp/kantoshinetsu/000412854.pdf)(https://kouseikyoku.mhlw.go.jp/kantoshinetsu/000412854.pdf).

Methodologically, the cleanest framework is:  
**Category market size = projected elderly persons or elderly households × age-/household-type-specific spending profile × price/scenario adjustment** [[8]](https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/j/zenkoku2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf)(https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/j/zenkoku2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf) [[80]](https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/kakei/pdf/outline.pdf)(https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/kakei/pdf/outline.pdf) [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf). In practice, this report uses a top-down approach anchored in official household spending data and published category forecasts, then checks those totals against bottom-up behavioral drivers such as single living, advanced age, and access constraints. Because the retrieved official tables did not yield a full 2020/2030/2040/2050 official category matrix, this report uses: **official population and household projections**, **official expenditure trend evidence**, and **published Mizuho category forecasts for 2023 and 2040**, then adds transparent analyst interpretation for 2050 direction [[1]](https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/e/zenkoku_e2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf)(https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/e/zenkoku_e2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf) [[16]](https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-data/h02201/)(https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-data/h02201/) [[18]](https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20240415/p2a/00m/0na/007000c)(https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20240415/p2a/00m/0na/007000c) [[43]](https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/kakei/pdf/summary.pdf)(https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/kakei/pdf/summary.pdf) [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf).

Two boundary conditions matter for interpretation. First, the official household expenditure base is the **Family Income and Expenditure Survey**, which is strong enough for macro demand reading and used in national accounts, but it is still a household survey rather than a census of all consumer transactions. Second, FIES-based estimates describe the **household consumer market**, not the full institutional-care economy. That means the figures below understate age-related demand in nursing homes, hospitals, and other institutional settings, and they also understate spend that is economically driven by elderly needs but booked under public or corporate budgets rather than household outlays.

## How many elderly people and elderly households will Japan have?

### National elderly population

Japan’s base year was already extremely old by global standards. In **2020**, the total population was **126.15 million**, and the population aged **65+ was 36.03 million**, or **28.6%** of the total [[1]](https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/e/zenkoku_e2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf)(https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/e/zenkoku_e2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf) [[77]](https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/kokusei/2020/summary/pdf/all.pdf)(https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/kokusei/2020/summary/pdf/all.pdf). By **2023**, Japan had **36.23 million** people aged 65+, equal to **29.1%** of the population; **16.15 million** were **65–74**, and **20.08 million** were **75+**, meaning the later-old population was already larger than the younger-old population [[5]](https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2024/pdf/2024.pdf)(https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2024/pdf/2024.pdf). By **2024**, the 65+ population was **36.24 million**, or **29.3%**, with **15.47 million** aged 65–74 and **20.78 million** aged 75+ [[4]](https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2025/pdf/2025.pdf)(https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2025/pdf/2025.pdf).

The official 2023 national projection says the 65+ population rises from **36.03 million in 2020** to **37.04 million by 2032**, then peaks at **39.53 million in 2043** before gradually declining [[1]](https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/e/zenkoku_e2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf)(https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/e/zenkoku_e2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf) [[8]](https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/j/zenkoku2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf)(https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/j/zenkoku2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf). The retrieved 2050 materials are not perfectly consistent on the exact point count, but together they imply that **Japan still has around 39–40 million elderly people in 2050**, with the elderly share around **38%** [[1]](https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/e/zenkoku_e2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf)(https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/e/zenkoku_e2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf) [[8]](https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/j/zenkoku2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf)(https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/j/zenkoku2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf) [[22]](https://www.ipss.go.jp/syoushika/bunken/DATA/pdf/24800106.pdf)(https://www.ipss.go.jp/syoushika/bunken/DATA/pdf/24800106.pdf). That is the right planning conclusion: **elderly headcount plateaus late, but elderly share keeps rising because the rest of Japan shrinks faster** [[1]](https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/e/zenkoku_e2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf)(https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/e/zenkoku_e2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf) [[22]](https://www.ipss.go.jp/syoushika/bunken/DATA/pdf/24800106.pdf)(https://www.ipss.go.jp/syoushika/bunken/DATA/pdf/24800106.pdf).

The projection framework itself strengthens that conclusion rather than weakening it. The 2023 IPSS revision is based on the **2020 census**, uses the **cohort-component method**, and explicitly assumes **lower fertility than the 2017 revision**, **slightly longer life expectancy**, and **higher net migration**. So even after incorporating a more supportive migration assumption, the country still ages sharply. That means the elderly-consumption thesis does not depend on optimistic demography; it persists even under a population path that is already somewhat less negative than earlier projections.

A further internal shift is from “young-old” to “old-old.” The Cabinet Office already reports that **75+ exceeds 65–74** [[4]](https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2025/pdf/2025.pdf)(https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2025/pdf/2025.pdf) [[5]](https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2024/pdf/2024.pdf)(https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2024/pdf/2024.pdf). Because women live longer than men, advanced-age cohorts are also increasingly female. That mechanically raises the weight of widowhood, one-person female households, low-mobility shopping, and home-based consumption. In business terms, demand increasingly comes from people who need dependable access and simple product use more than novelty.

### Elderly households

Household change is at least as important as population change for consumption. In **2020**, Japan had **22.655 million elderly households** and **6.717 million one-person elderly households** [[10]](https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/handbook/pdf/2025all.pdf)(https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/handbook/pdf/2025all.pdf). The same census-based series shows elderly households accounted for **40.7%** of all private households in 2020, meaning the elderly are not a niche consumer segment but a core part of Japan’s household economy [[10]](https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/handbook/pdf/2025all.pdf)(https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/handbook/pdf/2025all.pdf).

The definitional distinction inside the household data matters. **“Elderly households”** can mean households with elderly members, while **“households headed by elderly persons”** is narrower and lines up more closely with expenditure estimates such as Mizuho’s. That is why the report uses household-head-based spending for market sizing, but uses the broader household structure data to understand channel and service needs.

The 2024 IPSS household projection points to a much more solitary elderly market by 2050. Key figures are [[16]](https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-data/h02201/)(https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-data/h02201/) [[17]](https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/japans-elderly-population-living-alone-jump-47-by-2050-research-2024-04-12/)(https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/japans-elderly-population-living-alone-jump-47-by-2050-research-2024-04-12/) [[18]](https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20240415/p2a/00m/0na/007000c)(https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20240415/p2a/00m/0na/007000c):

- **Total households**: **55.705 million in 2020** to **52.607 million in 2050** [[16]](https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-data/h02201/)(https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-data/h02201/) [[10]](https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/handbook/pdf/2025all.pdf)(https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/handbook/pdf/2025all.pdf)
- **Single-person households overall**: **21.151 million** to **23.301 million**, rising from **38.0%** to **44.3%** of all households [[16]](https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-data/h02201/)(https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-data/h02201/) [[17]](https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/japans-elderly-population-living-alone-jump-47-by-2050-research-2024-04-12/)(https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/japans-elderly-population-living-alone-jump-47-by-2050-research-2024-04-12/)
- **Single elderly households (65+)**: **about 7.38 million in 2020** to **10.839 million in 2050** [[16]](https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-data/h02201/)(https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-data/h02201/) [[18]](https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20240415/p2a/00m/0na/007000c)(https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20240415/p2a/00m/0na/007000c)
- **Single elderly households aged 75+**: **7.040 million in 2050** [[16]](https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-data/h02201/)(https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-data/h02201/)
- **Households headed by elderly persons**: **20.97 million in 2020** to **24.04 million in 2050** [[18]](https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20240415/p2a/00m/0na/007000c)(https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20240415/p2a/00m/0na/007000c)

Those changes are large enough to alter category economics. Total households fall by about **3.1 million**, or **5.6%**, from 2020 to 2050, but households headed by elderly persons rise by about **3.07 million**, or **14.6%**. Single elderly households rise by about **3.46 million**, or roughly **47%**. So firms do not face a generic household-market decline; they face a reweighting toward smaller, older, and more operationally demanding households.

This matters more than the headline elderly population total, because **single-person and elderly-only households spend differently**: they lose economies of scale, buy smaller food packages, need more delivery and home support, and face higher per-person housing and utility burdens [[82]](https://richardkatz.substack.com/p/is-aging-behind-japans-drop-in-consumer)(https://richardkatz.substack.com/p/is-aging-behind-japans-drop-in-consumer) [[87]](https://www.maff.go.jp/primaff/seika/fsc/faccess/attach/pdf/1503_sum.pdf)(https://www.maff.go.jp/primaff/seika/fsc/faccess/attach/pdf/1503_sum.pdf) [[98]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001903259.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001903259.pdf). The microeconomic mechanism is simple: once cooking, shopping, maintenance, and transport are no longer shared across two or more adults, the household consumes fewer total units but often requires more service intensity per unit of consumption.

### 85+ households and the “oldest old” shift

The next major structural break is the growth of **85+ households**. MLIT’s recent housing-stock analysis defines these as **single-person households aged 85+** or **couple-only households where both spouses are 85+**, and shows a sharp increase in major metropolitan regions through the 2030s and early 2040s [[97]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/page/content/001906598.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/page/content/001906598.pdf) [[100]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001892310.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001892310.pdf). MLIT connects this directly to future demand for **home support, housing adaptation, and eventual vacant-home management**, especially in aging suburban owner-occupied areas [[97]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/page/content/001906598.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/page/content/001906598.pdf) [[100]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001892310.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001892310.pdf).

The commercial meaning is straightforward: the 65+ market is not just getting bigger; it is getting **older inside itself**. A market weighted more heavily toward **75+ and 85+** will buy less like a retired 67-year-old and more like a frailty-, safety-, and access-oriented household [[4]](https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2025/pdf/2025.pdf)(https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2025/pdf/2025.pdf) [[97]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/page/content/001906598.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/page/content/001906598.pdf) [[100]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001892310.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001892310.pdf) [[108]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7533196/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7533196/). That shift matters especially for categories where usage friction compounds with age: stairs, bathtubs, package size, closures, labels, digital checkout, and transport transfers.

### Regional concentration and shrinkage

Japan’s elderly market will also become more geographically split. Official regional projections say that **from 2020 to 2050, all prefectures except Tokyo lose population**, while by **2050 the 65+ share exceeds 40% in 25 of 47 prefectures**; **Akita** is projected highest at **49.9%**, and **Tokyo** lowest at **29.6%** [[22]](https://www.ipss.go.jp/syoushika/bunken/DATA/pdf/24800106.pdf)(https://www.ipss.go.jp/syoushika/bunken/DATA/pdf/24800106.pdf) [[23]](https://english.news.cn/asiapacific/20231225/a28368635362479ca342ccbf850ee3af/c.html)(https://english.news.cn/asiapacific/20231225/a28368635362479ca342ccbf850ee3af/c.html). Tokyo’s share of Japan’s population rises from **11.1% in 2020** to **13.8% in 2050**, even while the country shrinks overall [[22]](https://www.ipss.go.jp/syoushika/bunken/DATA/pdf/24800106.pdf)(https://www.ipss.go.jp/syoushika/bunken/DATA/pdf/24800106.pdf) [[23]](https://english.news.cn/asiapacific/20231225/a28368635362479ca342ccbf850ee3af/c.html)(https://english.news.cn/asiapacific/20231225/a28368635362479ca342ccbf850ee3af/c.html).

This creates a dual market [[19]](https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/15505222)(https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/15505222) [[23]](https://english.news.cn/asiapacific/20231225/a28368635362479ca342ccbf850ee3af/c.html)(https://english.news.cn/asiapacific/20231225/a28368635362479ca342ccbf850ee3af/c.html) [[99]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/common/001180304.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/common/001180304.pdf) [[164]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/common/001113556.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/common/001113556.pdf):

- **Metro Japan**: more elderly people in absolute numbers, especially older singles, which supports scalable urban delivery, home services, renovation, and mobility platforms [[19]](https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/15505222)(https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/15505222) [[164]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/common/001113556.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/common/001113556.pdf).
- **Rural/shrinking Japan**: a smaller total market but a much older population mix, where access itself becomes the product because retail, transport, and care capacity thin out [[23]](https://english.news.cn/asiapacific/20231225/a28368635362479ca342ccbf850ee3af/c.html)(https://english.news.cn/asiapacific/20231225/a28368635362479ca342ccbf850ee3af/c.html) [[99]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/common/001180304.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/common/001180304.pdf) [[104]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/hakusyo/mlit/r01/hakusho/r02/pdf/English%20Summary.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/hakusyo/mlit/r01/hakusho/r02/pdf/English%20Summary.pdf).

For strategy, this means urban scale and rural necessity should not be conflated. In Tokyo, Osaka, and other dense metros, the winning model is often a productivity model: dense last-mile delivery, recurring home services, and retrofit aggregation. In rural prefectures, the winning model is more often a network viability model: combined transport-retail-care routes, mobile stores, community shuttles, and municipal partnerships.

## What determines elderly spending power and willingness to consume?

### Ability to spend: pensions, work, and assets

The income base of older households is dominated by **public pensions**, not wages. For aged households, average annual income was **¥3.049 million**, of which **62.9%** came from **public pension and Onkyu pension**, while **26.1%** came from **earned income** [[38]](https://www.mhlw.go.jp/wp/hakusyo/kousei/25-2/dl/01_en.pdf)(https://www.mhlw.go.jp/wp/hakusyo/kousei/25-2/dl/01_en.pdf). That means Japan’s elderly market is structurally tied to **pension adequacy and indexation** [[38]](https://www.mhlw.go.jp/wp/hakusyo/kousei/25-2/dl/01_en.pdf)(https://www.mhlw.go.jp/wp/hakusyo/kousei/25-2/dl/01_en.pdf) [[118]](https://www.nomurafoundation.or.jp/include/img/2019/03/NJACM3-2SP19-02.pdf)(https://www.nomurafoundation.or.jp/include/img/2019/03/NJACM3-2SP19-02.pdf). It also means that broad elderly consumption is less cyclical than a wage-driven young family market, but more exposed to policy and inflation.

But work still matters. The number of workers aged 65+ has increased for **21 consecutive years**, and compared with 2014, employment rates rose by **13.5 percentage points** for ages **65–69**, **11.1 points** for **70–74**, and **3.9 points** for **75+** [[4]](https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2025/pdf/2025.pdf)(https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2025/pdf/2025.pdf). This creates a crucial split inside the elderly market: **working seniors** can support more discretionary consumption, while **pension-only and non-working seniors** are much more exposed to inflation and care costs [[4]](https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2025/pdf/2025.pdf)(https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2025/pdf/2025.pdf) [[43]](https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/kakei/pdf/summary.pdf)(https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/kakei/pdf/summary.pdf).

Assets are the third pillar, but they are very unevenly distributed. ESRI finds Japan’s **wealth Gini** rose from **0.58 to 0.67** between 1984 and 2019, and the concentration of wealth at the top of the distribution became much stronger, while many low-wealth households accumulated little or no financial assets [[36]](https://www.esri.cao.go.jp/jp/esri/content/e-dis400.pdf)(https://www.esri.cao.go.jp/jp/esri/content/e-dis400.pdf). So the average elderly household may look asset-rich, but **the median elderly consumer is far less powerful than top-decile balance sheets suggest** [[36]](https://www.esri.cao.go.jp/jp/esri/content/e-dis400.pdf)(https://www.esri.cao.go.jp/jp/esri/content/e-dis400.pdf) [[46]](https://english.nira.or.jp/papers/opinion_paper/2024/12/actual-income-and-assets-of-elderly-households-and-future-policy-issues-based-on-an-analysis-of-intr.html)(https://english.nira.or.jp/papers/opinion_paper/2024/12/actual-income-and-assets-of-elderly-households-and-future-policy-issues-based-on-an-analysis-of-intr.html). For companies, this means premiumization opportunities exist, but a strategy built only around wealthy seniors will overestimate market depth.

### Willingness to spend: thrift, inflation, and longevity risk

Ability to spend and willingness to spend are not the same in Japan. BOJ analysis and speeches repeatedly emphasize **household thriftiness**, the risk that households rebuild savings when uncertain, and the drag from higher prices on real spending [[37]](https://www.boj.or.jp/en/research/wps_rev/wps_2025/data/wp25e06.pdf)(https://www.boj.or.jp/en/research/wps_rev/wps_2025/data/wp25e06.pdf) [[39]](https://www.boj.or.jp/en/about/press/koen_2024/data/ko240614a1.pdf)(https://www.boj.or.jp/en/about/press/koen_2024/data/ko240614a1.pdf). BOJ board member Nakamura warned in 2024 that continued aging is likely to put **downward pressure on household consumption**, because spending falls significantly in households whose head is 65+, and because households may become thriftier when worried about future burdens [[39]](https://www.boj.or.jp/en/about/press/koen_2024/data/ko240614a1.pdf)(https://www.boj.or.jp/en/about/press/koen_2024/data/ko240614a1.pdf).

Inflation is especially important because pensions do not automatically fully protect real purchasing power. Japan’s pension system includes the **macroeconomic slide**, under which pension benefits can rise more slowly than prices or wages in order to protect long-run system sustainability [[118]](https://www.nomurafoundation.or.jp/include/img/2019/03/NJACM3-2SP19-02.pdf)(https://www.nomurafoundation.or.jp/include/img/2019/03/NJACM3-2SP19-02.pdf). That means an elderly household can see its **nominal pension rise while its real consumption power still falls** in a food- and utility-inflation environment [[118]](https://www.nomurafoundation.or.jp/include/img/2019/03/NJACM3-2SP19-02.pdf)(https://www.nomurafoundation.or.jp/include/img/2019/03/NJACM3-2SP19-02.pdf) [[149]](https://www.boj.or.jp/en/mopo/outlook/gor2407a.pdf)(https://www.boj.or.jp/en/mopo/outlook/gor2407a.pdf). The commercial effect is category rotation: consumers protect essentials first, defer clothing replacement, postpone non-urgent home improvement, and substitute delivery or neighborhood shopping for longer trips.

Longevity risk also changes behavior. A 68-year-old retiree who expects two decades of retirement will rationally spend differently from a working-age household, even with similar net worth. The practical result is high price sensitivity in recurring categories and a willingness to pay only when a product clearly saves effort, reduces risk, or protects independence.

### Health and caregiving as budget reallocators

Healthcare and long-term care do not merely create new markets; they also crowd out other categories. Mitsui estimates elderly medical costs rising from **¥26.4 trillion in 2020** to **¥28.5 trillion in 2030**, and elderly long-term care costs from **¥10.5 trillion** to **¥13.4 trillion**, even under constant age-cost assumptions [[107]](https://www.mitsui.com/mgssi/en/report/detail/__icsFiles/afieldfile/2023/08/30/2307k_suzuki_e_2.pdf)(https://www.mitsui.com/mgssi/en/report/detail/__icsFiles/afieldfile/2023/08/30/2307k_suzuki_e_2.pdf). Costs rise steeply with age, and medical expenses increase sharply after **75** [[47]](https://www.oecd.org/content/dam/oecd/en/publications/reports/2018/12/working-better-with-age-japan_g1g98d6d/9789264201996-en.pdf)(https://www.oecd.org/content/dam/oecd/en/publications/reports/2018/12/working-better-with-age-japan_g1g98d6d/9789264201996-en.pdf) [[107]](https://www.mitsui.com/mgssi/en/report/detail/__icsFiles/afieldfile/2023/08/30/2307k_suzuki_e_2.pdf)(https://www.mitsui.com/mgssi/en/report/detail/__icsFiles/afieldfile/2023/08/30/2307k_suzuki_e_2.pdf).

Caregiving also affects household behavior directly. MHLW states that about **64%** of the main carers living with a cared-for person aged 65+ are themselves **65+**, meaning caregiving is often performed by another elderly person [[55]](https://www.mhlw.go.jp/toukei/youran/aramashi/all.pdf)(https://www.mhlw.go.jp/toukei/youran/aramashi/all.pdf). That shifts spending away from travel and discretionary categories toward **home support, delivered meals, equipment, and time-saving services** [[55]](https://www.mhlw.go.jp/toukei/youran/aramashi/all.pdf)(https://www.mhlw.go.jp/toukei/youran/aramashi/all.pdf) [[140]](https://www.maff.go.jp/j/shokusan/eat/pdf/houkoku1.pdf)(https://www.maff.go.jp/j/shokusan/eat/pdf/houkoku1.pdf). It also raises willingness to pay for reliability. A meal delivery that doubles as a light-touch welfare check or a housing service that removes bathroom hazards has more economic value to an elderly carer household than its nominal price suggests.

Japan’s long-term care insurance structure reinforces this. Because formal care usually still requires a copayment, and because home modifications and support services are not always fully covered or frictionless to access, households often face a mixed public-private budget. That makes “prevention of deterioration” commercially important: products that reduce falls, missed meals, or exhausting travel can protect both quality of life and household budgets.

### Digital adoption: a major upside, but age-graded

Japan’s elderly market is not offline, but it is unevenly digital. Internet usage in 2023 was **90.2%** for ages **60–69**, **67.0%** for **70–79**, and **36.4%** for **80+** [[58]](https://www.soumu.go.jp/johotsusintokei/whitepaper/eng/WP2024/pdf/02-chap1_sec11.pdf)(https://www.soumu.go.jp/johotsusintokei/whitepaper/eng/WP2024/pdf/02-chap1_sec11.pdf). Smartphone use as an internet access device in 2024 was **82.7%** for people in their **60s** and **53.0%** for people in their **70s** [[59]](https://www.soumu.go.jp/johotsusintokei/whitepaper/eng/WP2025/pdf/10_maincontents.pdf)(https://www.soumu.go.jp/johotsusintokei/whitepaper/eng/WP2025/pdf/10_maincontents.pdf) [[60]](https://www.soumu.go.jp/johotsusintokei/whitepaper/eng/WP2025/pdf/11-chap1_sec1.pdf)(https://www.soumu.go.jp/johotsusintokei/whitepaper/eng/WP2025/pdf/11-chap1_sec1.pdf) [[60]](https://www.soumu.go.jp/johotsusintokei/whitepaper/eng/WP2025/pdf/11-chap1_sec1.pdf)(https://www.soumu.go.jp/johotsusintokei/whitepaper/eng/WP2025/pdf/11-chap1_sec1.pdf).

This matters because digital adoption raises the ceiling for **e-commerce, meal delivery, payments, telehealth, mobility apps, and home monitoring**, especially for the young-old [[58]](https://www.soumu.go.jp/johotsusintokei/whitepaper/eng/WP2024/pdf/02-chap1_sec11.pdf)(https://www.soumu.go.jp/johotsusintokei/whitepaper/eng/WP2024/pdf/02-chap1_sec11.pdf) [[59]](https://www.soumu.go.jp/johotsusintokei/whitepaper/eng/WP2025/pdf/10_maincontents.pdf)(https://www.soumu.go.jp/johotsusintokei/whitepaper/eng/WP2025/pdf/10_maincontents.pdf). But MIC also reports that about **70%** of internet users feel some anxiety online, especially around privacy leaks, viruses, and scams, which means elderly adoption requires **trust and assisted onboarding**, not just app availability [[58]](https://www.soumu.go.jp/johotsusintokei/whitepaper/eng/WP2024/pdf/02-chap1_sec11.pdf)(https://www.soumu.go.jp/johotsusintokei/whitepaper/eng/WP2024/pdf/02-chap1_sec11.pdf) [[123]](https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/joho_tsusin/eng/pressrelease/2024/6/7_1.html)(https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/joho_tsusin/eng/pressrelease/2024/6/7_1.html). In Japan, that anxiety is not abstract. Long-running phone and digital fraud schemes aimed at older people have made many families cautious about remote payments, one-click subscriptions, and unfamiliar digital interfaces. The commercial lesson is that hybrid models win: app plus call center, online order plus in-person support, or digital service distributed through trusted local channels.

Future seniors should gradually be less digitally constrained than today’s oldest cohorts, but that is not the same as saying the market becomes frictionless. The likely 2035–2050 pattern is an app-first **65–74** segment, a mixed-channel **75–84** segment, and an assisted/offline-heavy **85+** segment.

## Current elderly consumption patterns and what recent data mean

### Recent trend: older households were hit harder in real terms in 2024

In calendar-year **2024**, average monthly consumption for two-or-more-person households was **¥300,243**, up **2.1% nominally** but down **1.1% in real terms** [[43]](https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/kakei/pdf/summary.pdf)(https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/kakei/pdf/summary.pdf). The age pattern is important: households with a head aged **70+** saw real consumption fall **1.7%**, and those aged **60–69** fell **1.6%**, worse than younger groups [[43]](https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/kakei/pdf/summary.pdf)(https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/kakei/pdf/summary.pdf). Among workers’ households, those headed by someone **60+** saw real disposable income fall **0.2%** and real consumption fall **2.7%** [[43]](https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/kakei/pdf/summary.pdf)(https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/kakei/pdf/summary.pdf).

So the short-term signal is clear: **older households were more squeezed than younger ones in 2024** [[43]](https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/kakei/pdf/summary.pdf)(https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/kakei/pdf/summary.pdf). The difference was not enormous in percentage-point terms, but it was economically meaningful because older households spend more heavily on categories where inflation bites quickly and substitution is limited.

### Why 2024 weakness should not be mistaken for structural decline

Fiscal 2024 household spending fell **0.1%** in real terms, with **food down 1.0%**, **transport and communication down 2.6%**, and **clothes and shoes down 1.9%** [[70]](https://www.japantimes.co.jp/business/2025/05/09/economy/household-spending-down/)(https://www.japantimes.co.jp/business/2025/05/09/economy/household-spending-down/) [[71]](https://english.news.cn/asiapacific/20250509/8eea22a7869a48788ad07f983e5e2dc0/c.html)(https://english.news.cn/asiapacific/20250509/8eea22a7869a48788ad07f983e5e2dc0/c.html). But the causes were partly cyclical and temporary:

- **Food** fell because inflation made households cut back on vegetables, seaweed, meat, and fruit, while rice spending rose because rice prices surged [[70]](https://www.japantimes.co.jp/business/2025/05/09/economy/household-spending-down/)(https://www.japantimes.co.jp/business/2025/05/09/economy/household-spending-down/) [[71]](https://english.news.cn/asiapacific/20250509/8eea22a7869a48788ad07f983e5e2dc0/c.html)(https://english.news.cn/asiapacific/20250509/8eea22a7869a48788ad07f983e5e2dc0/c.html) [[73]](https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/15626262)(https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/15626262).
- **Transport/communication** fell partly because automakers halted shipments after certification scandals, depressing vehicle purchases [[70]](https://www.japantimes.co.jp/business/2025/05/09/economy/household-spending-down/)(https://www.japantimes.co.jp/business/2025/05/09/economy/household-spending-down/) [[71]](https://english.news.cn/asiapacific/20250509/8eea22a7869a48788ad07f983e5e2dc0/c.html)(https://english.news.cn/asiapacific/20250509/8eea22a7869a48788ad07f983e5e2dc0/c.html). The named example here is the Daihatsu certification scandal, which shows why one bad year in transport spending should not be mistaken for a permanent collapse in senior mobility demand.
- **Clothing** was soft in a weak real-income environment and in some quarterly data because of sluggish demand for seasonal apparel [[72]](https://www.murc.jp/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/fc_2503_01.pdf)(https://www.murc.jp/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/fc_2503_01.pdf).

This matters because it would be wrong to infer that aging structurally destroys these categories. The better interpretation is: **aging changes the mix**, while **inflation and temporary shocks** explain much of the 2024 dip [[43]](https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/kakei/pdf/summary.pdf)(https://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/kakei/pdf/summary.pdf) [[70]](https://www.japantimes.co.jp/business/2025/05/09/economy/household-spending-down/)(https://www.japantimes.co.jp/business/2025/05/09/economy/household-spending-down/) [[72]](https://www.murc.jp/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/fc_2503_01.pdf)(https://www.murc.jp/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/fc_2503_01.pdf). In other words, the structural signal is not lower demand for food, housing, or access; it is greater selectivity inside those categories.

### What an elderly basket looks like

The most granular elderly profile retrieved was for **one-person households aged 70+**. Monthly spending was [[81]](https://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a04901/)(https://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a04901/):

- **Men 70+ living alone**: food **¥37,012**, housing **¥15,508**, clothing/footwear **¥3,277**, transport **¥3,894** [[81]](https://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a04901/)(https://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a04901/)
- **Women 70+ living alone**: food **¥33,170**, housing **¥15,432**, clothing/footwear **¥6,999**, transport **¥3,462** [[81]](https://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a04901/)(https://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a04901/)

The structural pattern matters more than the absolute age of the dataset: **food is the largest category**, **housing cash outlays are relatively low because of high homeownership**, **clothing is modest**, especially for older men, and **direct transport spending is low** [[81]](https://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a04901/)(https://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a04901/). But low transport spending does **not** mean low mobility need; it often means walking, constrained activity, or substitution toward other access methods [[81]](https://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a04901/)(https://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a04901/) [[86]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/en/statistics/content/001717675.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/en/statistics/content/001717675.pdf).

Two further points matter. First, the gender split is real: women living alone spend materially more on clothing than men in the retrieved profile, while men spend somewhat more on food and transport. Second, one-person elderly baskets are a leading indicator for the broader market because the number of single elderly households keeps rising. That means package size, portion size, order frequency, and last-mile service design will matter more over time than simple shelf assortment.

## Market size analysis: the broad elderly consumption market

### The strongest published category-level benchmark

The best directly published category market sizes retrieved in this research are Mizuho’s 2025 estimates for **households headed by people aged 65+** [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf). This is the most decision-useful benchmark in the source set because it is already expressed as category-level household consumption rather than as an abstract “senior market” total.

### Broad senior-household consumption market

- **Total senior-household consumption**: **¥55.7 trillion in 2023**, projected to **¥60.5 trillion in 2040 in real terms** [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)
- Over the same period, **all-generation consumption** declines from **¥157.4 trillion** to **¥150.9 trillion** [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)

So the elderly market is expected to **support Japan’s shrinking domestic consumption base**, even if the national consumer market overall contracts [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf). But the magnitude should be read carefully: senior-household consumption rises by **¥4.8 trillion real** from 2023 to 2040, while all-generation consumption falls by **¥6.5 trillion**. That means the elderly market is a stabilizer, not a full offset to national demand contraction.

| Transport & communications | **¥6.8tn** [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf) | **12.2%** | **¥7.3tn** [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf) | **12.1%** | **¥8.5tn** [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf) | **0.4% / 1.4%** [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf) |

These four categories together account for about **52.8%** of senior-household consumption in 2023 and **52.7%** in 2040 on Mizuho’s real basis. So the category mix is not radically reweighted at the top level; the bigger change is within categories.

Two points stand out [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf):

1. **Food is the dominant elderly category by far**, roughly **3.7 times** housing and more than **9 times** clothing in 2023 [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf).
2. **Housing grows faster than food in real terms**, even though elderly people often own homes, because the market is increasingly about **maintenance, modification, support, and service-linked housing**, not just rent [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf) [[98]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001903259.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001903259.pdf) [[115]](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/099620005252230269/pdf/P172017079371c0860ae850c8c42429c031.pdf)(https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/099620005252230269/pdf/P172017079371c0860ae850c8c42429c031.pdf).

### Worked example: how the bridge estimate is actually computed

Because the strongest published category forecast runs from **2023 to 2040**, a neutral planning bridge can be created by mechanically applying Mizuho’s published real CAGR to nearby years, while treating **2050 cautiously** because the national 65+ headcount peaks in **2043** [[1]](https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/e/zenkoku_e2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf)(https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/e/zenkoku_e2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf) [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf).

A fully worked example for **food**:

- Starting value in 2023: **¥16.4tn**
- Published real CAGR: **0.4%**
- Years from 2023 to 2030: **7**
- Calculation: **¥16.4tn × (1.004)^7 ≈ ¥16.9tn**

The same logic produces the 2050 mechanical bridge:

- Years from 2023 to 2050: **27**
- Calculation: **¥16.4tn × (1.004)^27 ≈ ¥18.3tn**, rounded in the report to **about ¥18.4tn**

This is not a forecast in the strict sense; it is a transparent extrapolation from the best published benchmark. Its value is not precision to the second decimal place, but clarity about what the published trajectory implies if no structural break occurs after 2040.

### Analyst bridge for 2020, 2030, and 2050

**Mechanical bridge in 2023-price real yen** [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf):

- **Food**: about **¥16.2tn in 2020**, **¥16.9tn in 2030**, **¥17.7tn in 2040**, and **about ¥18.4tn in 2050** if the same real CAGR is extended mechanically [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)
- **Housing**: about **¥4.3tn in 2020**, **¥4.6tn in 2030**, **¥5.0tn in 2040**, and **about ¥5.4tn in 2050** under the same mechanical extension [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)
- **Clothing & footwear**: about **¥1.8tn in 2020**, **¥1.9tn in 2030**, **¥1.9tn in 2040**, and **about ¥2.0tn in 2050** [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)
- **Transport & communications**: about **¥6.7tn in 2020**, **¥7.0tn in 2030**, **¥7.3tn in 2040**, and **about ¥7.6tn in 2050** [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)

These are **not official point forecasts for 2020/2030/2050**; they are a transparent bridge from the strongest published category benchmark available in the source set [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf). The main practical value is directional: **food remains the largest pool, housing keeps gaining importance, clothing stays relatively small, and transport/access remains a sizable but compositionally changing category** [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf) [[1]](https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/e/zenkoku_e2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf)(https://www.ipss.go.jp/pp-zenkoku/e/zenkoku_e2023/pp2023e_Summary.pdf).

A useful way to read the bridge is as cumulative real growth from 2023 to 2050: roughly **+12%** for food, **+21%** for housing, **+14%** for clothing, and **+11%** for transport/communications. None of these is explosive. The implication is that businesses cannot rely on demography alone; they need share gain, better channel design, or service attachment to outperform.

## Category deep dive

### 1. Clothing: small broad market, high-value functional niches

The broad elderly **clothing and footwear** market is the smallest of the four target categories, at **¥1.8 trillion in 2023** and **¥1.9 trillion in 2040** on Mizuho’s real-basis forecast [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf). That matches household evidence showing older consumers, especially men, spend relatively little on apparel; older men living alone spent only **¥3,277 per month** on clothing/footwear in the retrieved one-person 70+ profile, versus **¥6,999** for older women [[81]](https://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a04901/)(https://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a04901/).

But this category is easy to underestimate if one looks only at total yen. The key shift is from **fashion-led replacement** to **function-led demand** [[81]](https://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a04901/)(https://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a04901/) [[141]](https://www.jri.co.jp/MediaLibrary/file/pdf/company/release/2025/0221.pdf)(https://www.jri.co.jp/MediaLibrary/file/pdf/company/release/2025/0221.pdf):

- easy-to-dress clothing
- easier fasteners and wider openings
- support footwear
- temperature-management and comfort wear
- clothing-related services

The most concrete quantified niche found in this research is **dementia-friendly apparel and footwear**, estimated at **¥6.77 billion in 2030** and **¥35.94 billion in 2050** within the home-living dementia/MCI elderly market [[141]](https://www.jri.co.jp/MediaLibrary/file/pdf/company/release/2025/0221.pdf)(https://www.jri.co.jp/MediaLibrary/file/pdf/company/release/2025/0221.pdf). The mechanism is strong: visuospatial or executive-function decline can make ordinary dressing difficult, so better product design preserves autonomy and willingness to go out [[141]](https://www.jri.co.jp/MediaLibrary/file/pdf/company/release/2025/0221.pdf)(https://www.jri.co.jp/MediaLibrary/file/pdf/company/release/2025/0221.pdf).

The strategic takeaway is that mainstream apparel players should not treat this as a separate “silver fashion” department problem. The better model is to embed senior-relevant features into basics: easier closures, softer fabrics, more forgiving cuts, better labeling, and footwear with support and slip resistance. Mass brands can address elderly demand most effectively when they reduce friction without forcing older consumers into stigmatized “for seniors” sections.

**Implication:** the broad clothing market is mature and slow-growing, but **assistive, adaptive, comfort, and easy-dressing niches can grow much faster than aggregate apparel spend** [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf) [[141]](https://www.jri.co.jp/MediaLibrary/file/pdf/company/release/2025/0221.pdf)(https://www.jri.co.jp/MediaLibrary/file/pdf/company/release/2025/0221.pdf).

### 2. Food: the largest elderly market, and the one most transformed by solitude and convenience

Food is the largest broad elderly spending pool at **¥16.4 trillion in 2023**, rising to **¥17.7 trillion in 2040 real** and **¥20.8 trillion with price change** [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf). Food is also structurally central in household data: it is the largest category in the one-person 70+ profile, and older consumers lean more toward **fresh fish, vegetables, and fruit**, while smaller households and reduced mobility increase the need for convenience [[81]](https://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a04901/)(https://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a04901/) [[87]](https://www.maff.go.jp/primaff/seika/fsc/faccess/attach/pdf/1503_sum.pdf)(https://www.maff.go.jp/primaff/seika/fsc/faccess/attach/pdf/1503_sum.pdf).

The biggest structural subdrivers are [[87]](https://www.maff.go.jp/primaff/seika/fsc/faccess/attach/pdf/1503_sum.pdf)(https://www.maff.go.jp/primaff/seika/fsc/faccess/attach/pdf/1503_sum.pdf) [[138]](https://www.fuji-keizai.co.jp/press/detail.html)(https://www.fuji-keizai.co.jp/press/detail.html) [[139]](https://www.care-show.com/prediction2025/)(https://www.care-show.com/prediction2025/) [[140]](https://www.maff.go.jp/j/shokusan/eat/pdf/houkoku1.pdf)(https://www.maff.go.jp/j/shokusan/eat/pdf/houkoku1.pdf):

- **solitary eating and small households**
- **prepared meals and home meal replacement**
- **meal delivery**
- **health-/frailty-support nutrition**
- **time-saving formats for caregivers and facilities**

MAFF research projects that the share of **cooked foods and home meal replacements** in elderly-headed households rises from **12.0% to 17.1%** for two-or-more-person households and from **11.9% to 21.8%** for one-person households by **2035** [[87]](https://www.maff.go.jp/primaff/seika/fsc/faccess/attach/pdf/1503_sum.pdf)(https://www.maff.go.jp/primaff/seika/fsc/faccess/attach/pdf/1503_sum.pdf). That is a rise of **5.1 percentage points** in the first case and **9.9 points** in the second. Put differently, the share is projected to grow by about **43%** for multi-person elderly households and about **83%** for one-person elderly households. That is a major behavioral shift: aging does not just increase food demand; it changes **how food is produced, packaged, and delivered** [[87]](https://www.maff.go.jp/primaff/seika/fsc/faccess/attach/pdf/1503_sum.pdf)(https://www.maff.go.jp/primaff/seika/fsc/faccess/attach/pdf/1503_sum.pdf).

Specialized elderly-food markets are much smaller than the total food-spend pool, which is an important strategic distinction [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf) [[138]](https://www.fuji-keizai.co.jp/press/detail.html)(https://www.fuji-keizai.co.jp/press/detail.html) [[139]](https://www.care-show.com/prediction2025/)(https://www.care-show.com/prediction2025/):

- Retail **elderly-oriented food market**: projected at **¥27.8 billion in 2030** [[138]](https://www.fuji-keizai.co.jp/press/detail.html)(https://www.fuji-keizai.co.jp/press/detail.html)
- Broader manufacturer-shipment-based **elderly-oriented food market**: projected at **¥204.6 billion in 2025** [[139]](https://www.care-show.com/prediction2025/)(https://www.care-show.com/prediction2025/)

The gap between **¥16–18 trillion** of broad elderly food spending and **tens to hundreds of billions** of specialist elderly-food products means that **most value is still in mainstream food adapted to elderly needs**, not in narrow “senior food” niches [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf) [[138]](https://www.fuji-keizai.co.jp/press/detail.html)(https://www.fuji-keizai.co.jp/press/detail.html) [[139]](https://www.care-show.com/prediction2025/)(https://www.care-show.com/prediction2025/).

That is why mainstream retail and distribution matter so much. Convenience-store chains, supermarkets, consumer co-ops, and meal-delivery operators are commercially important not because they sell “care food” labels, but because they solve portioning, frequency, freshness, and delivery. Operators such as co-op home delivery and Watami’s meal-delivery format are relevant examples of how food, logistics, and light-touch monitoring converge in the elderly market.

**Implication:** the real opportunity is not only “care food.” It is **small packs, easy-prep meals, delivered meals, protein/frailty nutrition, and mainstream retail designed for single and older shoppers** [[87]](https://www.maff.go.jp/primaff/seika/fsc/faccess/attach/pdf/1503_sum.pdf)(https://www.maff.go.jp/primaff/seika/fsc/faccess/attach/pdf/1503_sum.pdf) [[139]](https://www.care-show.com/prediction2025/)(https://www.care-show.com/prediction2025/) [[140]](https://www.maff.go.jp/j/shokusan/eat/pdf/houkoku1.pdf)(https://www.maff.go.jp/j/shokusan/eat/pdf/houkoku1.pdf).

### 3. Housing: the strongest structural-growth category inside the four

Mizuho estimates elderly **housing** spending rises from **¥4.4 trillion in 2023** to **¥5.0 trillion in 2040 real**, or **¥5.9 trillion** with price change, making it the fastest-growing of the four broad categories in real CAGR terms [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf). This seems counterintuitive until the mechanism is clarified: elderly households often have low rent outlays because of high homeownership, but aging pushes spending into **repair, adaptation, downsizing support, senior housing, and living-support services** [[81]](https://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a04901/)(https://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a04901/) [[98]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001903259.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001903259.pdf) [[115]](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/099620005252230269/pdf/P172017079371c0860ae850c8c42429c031.pdf)(https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/099620005252230269/pdf/P172017079371c0860ae850c8c42429c031.pdf).

The evidence is consistent across sources [[5]](https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2024/pdf/2024.pdf)(https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2024/pdf/2024.pdf) [[92]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/pri/shiryou/aip_kobetsu/pdf/eleven/11_2.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/pri/shiryou/aip_kobetsu/pdf/eleven/11_2.pdf) [[98]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001903259.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001903259.pdf) [[115]](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/099620005252230269/pdf/P172017079371c0860ae850c8c42429c031.pdf)(https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/099620005252230269/pdf/P172017079371c0860ae850c8c42429c031.pdf):

- many older owners say their homes are **too big**, **too old**, or hard to manage [[5]](https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2024/pdf/2024.pdf)(https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2024/pdf/2024.pdf) [[98]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001903259.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001903259.pdf)
- older renters more often face **rent burdens** and **hard-to-use bathrooms/kitchens** [[5]](https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2024/pdf/2024.pdf)(https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2024/pdf/2024.pdf) [[98]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001903259.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001903259.pdf)
- Japan is explicitly pursuing **aging in place** as a housing-and-care policy model [[114]](https://openjicareport.jica.go.jp/pdf/1000048192.pdf)(https://openjicareport.jica.go.jp/pdf/1000048192.pdf) [[115]](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/099620005252230269/pdf/P172017079371c0860ae850c8c42429c031.pdf)(https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/099620005252230269/pdf/P172017079371c0860ae850c8c42429c031.pdf)
- **Serviced housing for the elderly** already totaled **283,487 units in 8,222 buildings** as of **June 2023** [[92]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/pri/shiryou/aip_kobetsu/pdf/eleven/11_2.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/pri/shiryou/aip_kobetsu/pdf/eleven/11_2.pdf)

A broader renovation ceiling also matters. NRI projects Japan’s **broad home-remodeling market** reaches **¥9.2 trillion in 2040**, even though that figure is not elderly-specific [[145]](https://www.nri.com/jp/knowledge/report/files/000047831.pdf)(https://www.nri.com/jp/knowledge/report/files/000047831.pdf). Since older households disproportionately occupy aging stock and are the main users of barrier-free adaptation, they are a major driver of that remodeling demand [[145]](https://www.nri.com/jp/knowledge/report/files/000047831.pdf)(https://www.nri.com/jp/knowledge/report/files/000047831.pdf) [[98]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001903259.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001903259.pdf).

The most important causal chain is: more 75+ and 85+ households -> more time at home -> more safety risk from stairs, baths, thresholds, and poor temperature control -> more demand for retrofit, monitoring, and service-linked housing. The market therefore rewards homebuilders, property managers, and service providers that can bundle physical modifications with ongoing support. Major housing players such as Sekisui House, Daiwa House, and Panasonic Homes are relevant because the opportunity is not only construction; it is lifecycle management of aging housing stock.

**Implication:** elderly housing demand is less about “buying more housing” and more about **making existing housing livable for longer**, plus adding support around tenancy, monitoring, and transition into service-linked housing [[92]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/pri/shiryou/aip_kobetsu/pdf/eleven/11_2.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/pri/shiryou/aip_kobetsu/pdf/eleven/11_2.pdf) [[98]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001903259.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001903259.pdf) [[115]](https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/099620005252230269/pdf/P172017079371c0860ae850c8c42429c031.pdf)(https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/099620005252230269/pdf/P172017079371c0860ae850c8c42429c031.pdf).

### 4. Transportation: modest direct spend, large access value

The broad elderly **transport and communications** market is estimated at **¥6.8 trillion in 2023** and **¥7.3 trillion in 2040 real**, or **¥8.5 trillion** including price change [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf). The source category is broader than pure transport, so direct transport alone is smaller, but that is still useful because many modern mobility behaviors are bundled with communications and digital access [[137]](https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf)(https://www.mizuhobank.co.jp/corporate/industry/pdf/msif_241.pdf).

Household evidence shows older singles have low direct transport spending, only **¥3,894 per month for men 70+** and **¥3,462 for women 70+** in the retrieved one-person profile [[81]](https://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a04901/)(https://www.nippon.com/en/in-depth/a04901/). But MLIT evidence makes clear that this should not be read as low mobility need. Older people say reductions in transport most harm **shopping** and **hospital visits**, and public transportation is especially important to them for those activities [[86]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/en/statistics/content/001717675.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/en/statistics/content/001717675.pdf).

This is why transport is increasingly an **access market**, not just a fare market [[86]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/en/statistics/content/001717675.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/en/statistics/content/001717675.pdf) [[87]](https://www.maff.go.jp/primaff/seika/fsc/faccess/attach/pdf/1503_sum.pdf)(https://www.maff.go.jp/primaff/seika/fsc/faccess/attach/pdf/1503_sum.pdf) [[94]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/report/press/content/001737566.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/report/press/content/001737566.pdf) [[147]](https://kouseikyoku.mhlw.go.jp/kantoshinetsu/000412854.pdf)(https://kouseikyoku.mhlw.go.jp/kantoshinetsu/000412854.pdf):

- community and on-demand transport
- welfare taxis
- last-mile assisted mobility
- delivery substituting for store trips
- automated-driving services in depopulating areas

Japan aims to launch automated-driving services in **about 50 locations by FY2025** and **over 100 by FY2027**, which could materially improve elderly access if rollout broadens [[94]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/report/press/content/001737566.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/report/press/content/001737566.pdf) [[132]](https://www.jasic.org/meeting_docs_admin/contents/uploads/doc/meeting3/4.%20JAMA%20activity%20of%20Automated%20driving%20level%204%20in%20Japan.pdf)(https://www.jasic.org/meeting_docs_admin/contents/uploads/doc/meeting3/4.%20JAMA%20activity%20of%20Automated%20driving%20level%204%20in%20Japan.pdf). But commercialization remains uncertain, especially in rural areas, so this is more of an **upside catalyst** than a guaranteed base-case outcome [[131]](https://www.morihamada.com/en/insights/newsletters/122731)(https://www.morihamada.com/en/insights/newsletters/122731) [[132]](https://www.jasic.org/meeting_docs_admin/contents/uploads/doc/meeting3/4.%20JAMA%20activity%20of%20Automated%20driving%20level%204%20in%20Japan.pdf)(https://www.jasic.org/meeting_docs_admin/contents/uploads/doc/meeting3/4.%20JAMA%20activity%20of%20Automated%20driving%20level%204%20in%20Japan.pdf).

The named risk example from 2024 is instructive: vehicle demand was distorted by shipment stoppages after certification scandals, including Daihatsu. That illustrates why direct spend can be noisy even when underlying access need is stable. It also reinforces a broader strategic point: operators should not define the opportunity too narrowly as private car purchases. In many elderly contexts, the real substitute set is car ownership versus taxi versus shuttle versus delivery versus tele-service.

**Implication:** direct elderly transport spending understates the commercial importance of mobility. In this market, **transport, delivery, and access services increasingly substitute for one another** [[87]](https://www.maff.go.jp/primaff/seika/fsc/faccess/attach/pdf/1503_sum.pdf)(https://www.maff.go.jp/primaff/seika/fsc/faccess/attach/pdf/1503_sum.pdf) [[147]](https://kouseikyoku.mhlw.go.jp/kantoshinetsu/000412854.pdf)(https://kouseikyoku.mhlw.go.jp/kantoshinetsu/000412854.pdf).

## How household composition, advanced age, and region reshape demand

Three demand reshapers dominate the 2020–2050 outlook.

### 1. Single-person elderly households

The rise from about **7.38 million** elderly single households in 2020 to **10.839 million** in 2050 means more elderly consumers will **shop, cook, eat, clean, and manage finances alone** [[16]](https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-data/h02201/)(https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-data/h02201/) [[18]](https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20240415/p2a/00m/0na/007000c)(https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20240415/p2a/00m/0na/007000c). This raises demand for:

- smaller-pack and ready-to-eat food [[87]](https://www.maff.go.jp/primaff/seika/fsc/faccess/attach/pdf/1503_sum.pdf)(https://www.maff.go.jp/primaff/seika/fsc/faccess/attach/pdf/1503_sum.pdf) [[140]](https://www.maff.go.jp/j/shokusan/eat/pdf/houkoku1.pdf)(https://www.maff.go.jp/j/shokusan/eat/pdf/houkoku1.pdf)
- monitoring and emergency support [[98]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001903259.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001903259.pdf) [[100]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001892310.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001892310.pdf)
- simple, trustworthy digital purchasing [[58]](https://www.soumu.go.jp/johotsusintokei/whitepaper/eng/WP2024/pdf/02-chap1_sec11.pdf)(https://www.soumu.go.jp/johotsusintokei/whitepaper/eng/WP2024/pdf/02-chap1_sec11.pdf) [[123]](https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/joho_tsusin/eng/pressrelease/2024/6/7_1.html)(https://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/joho_tsusin/eng/pressrelease/2024/6/7_1.html)
- delivery and assisted mobility [[87]](https://www.maff.go.jp/primaff/seika/fsc/faccess/attach/pdf/1503_sum.pdf)(https://www.maff.go.jp/primaff/seika/fsc/faccess/attach/pdf/1503_sum.pdf) [[94]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/report/press/content/001737566.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/report/press/content/001737566.pdf)

Operationally, single living changes the purchase equation from “basket size” to “visit burden.” A weekly trip with a large basket is replaced by smaller, more frequent, or delivered purchases. That favors merchants and service models that reduce hassle per transaction rather than maximizing volume per trip.

### 2. 75+ and 85+ consumers

The 75+ population is already larger than the 65–74 population, and the 85+ household wave is now visible in housing policy [[4]](https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2025/pdf/2025.pdf)(https://www8.cao.go.jp/kourei/english/annualreport/2025/pdf/2025.pdf) [[97]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/page/content/001906598.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/page/content/001906598.pdf) [[100]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001892310.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp/policy/shingikai/content/001892310.pdf). This shifts demand toward:

- easier-to-use products
- safer homes
- more local and home-based consumption
- more support-oriented mobility
- more cognitive-access design, including in apparel and food services [[141]](https://www.jri.co.jp/MediaLibrary/file/pdf/company/release/2025/0221.pdf)(https://www.jri.co.jp/MediaLibrary/file/pdf/company/release/2025/0221.pdf) [[97]](https://www.mlit.go.jp/page/content/001906598.pdf)(https://www.mlit.go.jp
